flashcards.Back: A standard IPv6 address is 128 bits long, providing a massive address space of approximately $3.4 \times 10^{38}$ unique identifiers.
IPv6 addresses are written in hexadecimal format, divided into eight groups of four digits (quartets) separated by colons.
An IPv6 rule that allows any leading zeros in a 16-bit quartet to be removed to simplify the address (e.g., '0db8' becomes 'db8').
A rule that allows a single contiguous sequence of all-zero quartets to be replaced by a double colon (::). This can only be done once per address.
The leading bits of an IPv6 address that identify the network or subnet. it is analogous to the network portion of an IPv4 address.
The 64-bit portion of an IPv6 address that uniquely identifies a specific interface on a subnet. it occupies the last half of the 128-bit address.
The portion of a Global Unicast Address assigned to an organization by an ISP or registry, typically the first 48 bits.
The 16-bit field in a standard /64 IPv6 address that follows the global routing prefix, allowing for the creation of 65,536 subnets.
A globally unique and routable IPv6 address, equivalent to a public IPv4 address. gua addresses currently begin with the binary prefix 001 (2000::/3).
An IPv6 address used for communication only within a single local link. These addresses always start with the prefix fe80::/10.
An IPv6 address intended for use within an organization's internal network, similar to private IPv4. They use the prefix fc00::/7 (commonly fd00::/8).
An IPv6 address used to send a single packet to multiple destinations simultaneously. These addresses always begin with the prefix ff00::/8.
An IPv6 address assigned to multiple interfaces (typically on different nodes). A packet sent to this address is delivered to the 'nearest' interface.
A method to automatically generate a 64-bit Interface ID from a 48-bit MAC address by inserting 'fffe' in the middle and inverting the 7th bit.
Stateless Address Autoconfiguration. A mechanism that allows an IPv6 host to configure its own address using Router Advertisement messages.
A configuration where a host uses SLAAC for its address but queries a DHCPv6 server for additional info like DNS server addresses.
A configuration where a DHCPv6 server centrally manages and assigns IPv6 addresses to clients, similar to traditional DHCP in IPv4.
The reserved IPv6 address 0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1, abbreviated as ::1/128. it is used by a host to send packets to itself for testing.
The all-zeros IPv6 address (::/128) used as a source address by a host before it has been assigned a unique address.
A mandatory IPv6 process where a host verifies that its newly configured address is not already in use on the local link.
An ICMPv6-based protocol suite that replaces ARP. it handles MAC address resolution, router discovery, and link-layer health checks.
A periodic ICMPv6 message sent by routers to inform hosts of the network's presence and provide configuration parameters like the prefix.
An ICMPv6 message sent by a host upon joining a network to immediately request configuration information from any available local routers.
An ICMPv6 message used by a host to determine the MAC address of a neighbor or to verify that a neighbor is still reachable.
A response to a Neighbor Solicitation message that provides the requested MAC address or announces a change in the host's link-layer info.
A special multicast address used in IPv6 neighbor discovery. it is formed by the prefix ff02::1:ff00:0/104 + the last 24 bits of the unicast address.
An IPv6 address that targets every IPv6-enabled device on the local link. it is the equivalent of an IPv4 broadcast.
An IPv6 address used to communicate with all routers on a local segment, primarily for Router Solicitation messages.
A transition strategy where network devices run both IPv4 and IPv6 protocol stacks simultaneously to ensure compatibility with both networks.
A transition method that encapsulates IPv6 packets inside IPv4 headers so they can be transported across an existing IPv4-only infrastructure.
